title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

Explain the purpose and structure of the IMAG simulation
Navigate the Cesim Invest platform and locate key decision screens
Describe the round-by-round investment process
Assign team roles and agree on a collaborative workflow
Understand how the simulation is graded

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
What is IMAG?

An Erasmus+ project connecting universities across Europe
A structured investment simulation using the Cesim Invest platform
Six lessons that take you from blank mandate to full portfolio review
Designed to mirror real-world investment management processes

→ Content: project background, partner institutions, learning goals.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Why a Simulation?

Safe environment to make — and learn from — real portfolio decisions
Immediate feedback through performance metrics each round
Exposure to the full investment cycle: research → decision → review

→ Content: simulation vs. classroom theory; what you can and cannot do in Cesim.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Platform Overview

Browser-based; no installation required
Key navigation areas: Economic Outlook · Strategy · Markets · Analyst · Results
Data updates each round — decisions are locked at the round deadline

→ Content: annotated screenshot of the platform home screen.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
The Asset Universe

Equities (domestic & international)
Fixed income (government & corporate bonds)
Cash & money market instruments
Alternative assets (commodities, real estate, digital assets)
Currency exposure

→ Content: screenshot of the Markets screen with asset list.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Key Performance Indicators



KPI
What it measures




Portfolio return
Absolute & relative to benchmark


Sharpe ratio
Risk-adjusted return


Tracking error
Deviation from benchmark


ESG score
Sustainability integration


Drawdown
Maximum loss from peak



→ Content: where to find each KPI in Cesim Results.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Round Structure

Each round = one quarter (or one year, depending on setup)
Sequence: read data → form views → submit decisions → observe results
Decisions carry over; you must actively rebalance

→ Content: round timeline diagram.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Decision Deadlines & Workflow

Deadlines are fixed — late submissions are not accepted
Recommended team workflow: research → debate → agree → submit
Document your reasoning (feeds into the feedback loop, Lesson 6)

→ Content: sample team calendar / decision log template.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Scoring & Grading

→ Content: grading rubric (risk-adjusted return, ESG, IPS consistency, reflection quality).

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Suggested Role Allocation



Role
Responsibility




Chief Investment Officer
Final decision sign-off, IPS owner


Macro Analyst
Economic outlook, scenario design


Portfolio Manager
SAA/TAA, risk budgeting


ESG Analyst
Sustainability scoring, impact assessment


Performance Analyst
Results review, attribution



→ Adapt roles to team size. Rotate if possible.

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Key Takeaways

IMAG replicates the full investment management process in six lessons
Every decision should be traceable to your IPS (Lesson 3)
Document your reasoning — you will review it in Lesson 6

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Simulation Task — Lesson 1






Before next lesson



Log in to Cesim Invest and explore all five navigation sections
Agree on team roles and a communication channel
Read: Introduction chapter in the teaching materials

title: Lesson 1: Introduction & Simulation Setup
text:
Further Reading

Teaching materials: Introduction chapter
Engine documentation: asset universe and KPI definitions

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

Classify the main asset classes available in IMAG and explain their risk-return characteristics
Describe how macro drivers influence asset prices across the cycle
Integrate ESG criteria into asset class analysis
Map the investable universe to the Cesim Invest platform

Teaching chapters: Markets & Asset Classes · Sustainable Investing

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
The Investable Universe

Four broad asset classes: Equities · Fixed Income · Alternatives · Cash
Each has a distinct risk-return profile, liquidity, and ESG footprint
The IMAG universe is a subset of the global investable universe

→ Content: table of asset classes with expected return, volatility, and correlation.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Equities

Represent ownership in firms; residual claim on cash flows
Priced by: earnings growth, discount rate, sentiment
Key dimensions: geography, sector, size, style (value vs. growth)
ESG dimension: governance quality, climate exposure

→ Content: equity risk premium history; sector heatmap.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Fixed Income

Government bonds: risk-free benchmark; duration & yield curve risk
Corporate bonds: credit spread as compensation for default risk
Yield curve dynamics: steepening, flattening, inversion
Cash & money market: liquidity reserve; real rate exposure

→ Content: yield curve diagram; credit spread history.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Alternative Assets & Digital Assets

Commodities: inflation hedge; supply-demand dynamics
Real estate: income + capital appreciation; illiquidity premium
Digital assets: high volatility; low correlation (historically)
Role in a diversified portfolio: diversification benefit vs. complexity cost

→ Content: correlation matrix across asset classes.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Currencies & FX Risk

Multi-currency portfolio: returns affected by exchange rate moves
Hedging choices: full hedge, partial hedge, unhedged
Currency as an active return source vs. unintended risk

→ Content: FX impact illustration on a cross-border equity position.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
The Macro Framework

Four macro regimes: Expansion · Slowdown · Recession · Recovery
Each regime has characteristic asset class returns
Regime identification: leading indicators, yield curve, PMI, credit spreads

→ Content: regime wheel diagram; asset class returns by regime table.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Key Risk Premia



Premium
Driver
Asset class




Equity risk premium
Growth, earnings
Equities


Term premium
Duration risk
Long bonds


Credit spread
Default risk
Corp. bonds


Inflation premium
Real rate uncertainty
TIPS, commodities


Illiquidity premium
Lock-up cost
Alternatives



→ Content: historical estimates for each premium.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Valuation Signals

Equities: P/E, CAPE, earnings yield vs. bond yield (Fed model)
Bonds: real yield, breakeven inflation
Credit: option-adjusted spread vs. historical average
Valuation is a poor short-term timer but a strong long-run anchor

→ Content: current valuation dashboard from Cesim Economic Outlook screen.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
The ESG Framework

Environmental: climate risk, carbon footprint, resource use
Social: labour standards, supply chain, community impact
Governance: board structure, executive pay, shareholder rights

→ Content: ESG rating methodology overview; data providers.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Integration Approaches



Approach
Description




Exclusion
Remove sectors/companies (tobacco, weapons, etc.)


ESG integration
Adjust return/risk estimates for ESG factors


Best-in-class
Select top ESG scorers within each sector


Impact investing
Target measurable positive outcomes


Engagement
Active ownership to improve practices

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
ESG in the IMAG Simulation

ESG score is a graded KPI — it affects your final result
Data available in Cesim: per-asset ESG ratings
Trade-off: higher ESG score may constrain return maximisation
You must justify your ESG approach in the IPS (Lesson 3)

→ Content: screenshot of ESG data in Cesim; worked example of ESG-adjusted allocation.

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Key Takeaways

Asset class choice drives the bulk of long-run portfolio returns
Macro regime awareness improves timing and risk management
ESG is not just a constraint — it is a source of risk-adjusted alpha

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Simulation Task — Lesson 2






Before next lesson



In Cesim, open the Markets screen and record the available assets
Identify each asset’s asset class, geography, and ESG rating
Form a preliminary view on the current macro regime (use Economic Outlook)
Read: Markets & Asset Classes and Sustainable Investing chapters

title: Lesson 2: Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
text:
Further Reading

Chapter: Markets, Asset Classes, Instruments & Currencies
Chapter: Sustainable and Impact Investing
Chapter: Drivers of Asset Prices

title: IMAG Teaching Slides
text:
INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT GAME (IMAG)
    
      Project
      Team
      Outcomes
      Events
      Game
      Contact
    
  





These slide decks accompany the IMAG Teaching Materials and are designed for six 90-minute lessons. Each deck is self-contained and links back to the relevant chapters for deeper reading.



Lesson
Topic
Key Simulation Task




Lesson 1
Introduction & Simulation Setup
Get started with Cesim Invest


Lesson 2
Markets, Asset Classes & ESG
Map the investable universe


Lesson 3
Client Discovery & Investment Policy Statement
Draft the IPS


Lesson 4
Strategic Asset Allocation
Build & submit the SAA


Lesson 5
Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
Tactical tilts & security picks


Lesson 6
Performance Measurement & Feedback Loop
Analyse results & iterate

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

Derive long-run capital market assumptions (returns, risks, correlations)
Design macro scenarios and assign probabilities
Construct an efficient SAA consistent with your IPS
Translate the SAA into Cesim Invest decision inputs

Teaching chapters: Drivers of Asset Prices · Data, Forecasting & Scenario Design · Strategic Research Toolkit · Strategic Asset Allocation

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
What Are Capital Market Assumptions?

Long-run (10-year) forecasts of: expected returns · volatility · correlations
The foundation of every SAA decision
Sources: historical data, factor models, building-block approach, survey consensus
Must be consistent with your macro view and the IPS return objective

→ Content: CMA table for IMAG asset classes (to be filled with Cesim data).

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
The Building-Block Approach
\[\text{Expected Return} = r_f + \text{Risk Premium}\]



Asset class
Components




Equities
Risk-free + equity risk premium + regional premium


Corp. bonds
Risk-free + term premium + credit spread


Commodities
Risk-free + convenience yield + roll return



→ Content: calibrated estimates using current Cesim Economic Outlook data.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Estimating Volatility & Correlations

Historical volatility: rolling 3- and 5-year windows
Correlation instability: correlations rise during crises (diversification fails when needed)
Shrinkage estimators: blend historical with prior (reduces estimation error)
Correlation matrix visualisation (heatmap)

→ Content: worked example with IMAG asset data.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Why Scenarios?

Point forecasts are always wrong — scenarios capture uncertainty
Force structured thinking about tail risks
Enable stress-testing of the SAA before committing
Required by many regulatory frameworks (IORP II, Solvency II)

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
A Three-Scenario Framework



Scenario
Probability
Description




Base case
60%
Moderate growth, inflation normalising


Upside
25%
Strong recovery, risk-on environment


Downside
15%
Recession, credit stress, flight to quality



→ Content: fill with IMAG-specific macro assumptions per scenario.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Scenario Returns by Asset Class
→ Content: table of expected returns per asset class under each scenario; probability-weighted expected return column.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Stress Testing the SAA

Apply downside scenario to candidate SAA
Check: does portfolio breach IPS risk constraints?
If yes: reduce risk → revisit return objective → update IPS

→ Content: stress test template; link to Cesim back-testing tools if available.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Mean-Variance Optimisation

Classic Markowitz framework: maximise return for a given level of risk
Inputs: expected returns, volatility, correlation matrix
Output: efficient frontier — the set of optimal portfolios

→ Content: efficient frontier diagram; locate the IPS-consistent portfolio.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Practical Constraints on the Optimiser

No short-selling (long-only constraint)
Minimum and maximum weights per asset class (IPS constraints)
Turnover limit (transaction cost management)
ESG constraint: minimum portfolio ESG score







Tip


Unconstrained optimisation produces extreme corner solutions. Always impose realistic bounds.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
section: From Efficient Frontier to Strategic Weights
text:
From Efficient Frontier to Strategic Weights

Select the portfolio that maximises expected return subject to the IPS risk budget
Round weights to practical increments (e.g. nearest 5%)
Check consistency with IPS: return target, risk limit, ESG score, liquidity

→ Content: final SAA weights table for IMAG (to be completed by each team).

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Rebalancing Policy

Calendar rebalancing: restore weights monthly/quarterly regardless of drift
Threshold rebalancing: trigger when any weight drifts > X% from target
Transaction costs vs. tracking error trade-off
Document policy in IPS

→ Content: comparison of rebalancing strategies; recommendation for IMAG.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Entering Your SAA in Cesim

Navigate to: Strategy → Strategic Asset Allocation
Enter target weights for each eligible asset class
The system enforces that weights sum to 100%

→ Content: annotated screenshot of the Cesim SAA input screen.

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
What Happens Each Round

Your SAA is the long-run anchor — you will deviate from it tactically in Lesson 5
Cesim calculates benchmark return based on your SAA weights
Active return = portfolio return minus SAA benchmark return

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Key Takeaways

The SAA drives 80–90% of long-run portfolio return variation
Scenario analysis prevents over-confidence in point forecasts
Every SAA weight must be justified by your IPS and CMA

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Simulation Task — Lesson 4






Deliverable: Your SAA



Build your capital market assumptions table (use Cesim Economic Outlook + own research)
Design three scenarios with probabilities
Run mean-variance optimisation (use provided template or Excel)
Define final SAA weights — consistent with your IPS
Submit SAA in Cesim before the round deadline

title: Lesson 4: Strategic Asset Allocation
text:
Further Reading

Chapter: Drivers of Asset Prices
Chapter: Data, Forecasting & Scenario Design
Chapter: Strategic Research Toolkit
Chapter: Strategic Asset Allocation

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

Calculate and interpret risk-adjusted performance metrics
Decompose portfolio return into asset allocation and security selection effects
Conduct a structured post-mortem of your investment decisions
Close the feedback loop: update your IPS and views for the next round

Teaching chapters: Performance Measurement & Attribution · Feedback Loop

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Return Calculation

Holding period return: \((P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t) / P_{t-1}\)
Time-weighted return (TWR): eliminates impact of external cash flows — the industry standard
Money-weighted return (MWR / IRR): reflects timing of investor cash flows — useful for client reporting
Distinguish: gross of fees vs. net of fees

→ Content: worked return calculation using IMAG Results screen.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Risk-Adjusted Performance Metrics








Metric
Formula
What it tells you




Sharpe ratio
\((R_p - R_f) / \sigma_p\)
Return per unit of total risk


Sortino ratio
\((R_p - R_f) / \sigma_{downside}\)
Return per unit of downside risk


Information ratio
\((R_p - R_b) / \text{TE}\)
Active return per unit of active risk


Calmar ratio
Annualised return / Max drawdown
Return per unit of drawdown


Maximum drawdown
Peak-to-trough loss
Worst-case loss



→ Content: your team’s metrics from Cesim Results.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Benchmark Comparison

Did you outperform or underperform the benchmark?
Decompose: how much came from asset allocation vs. security selection vs. interaction?
Distinguish: good decision vs. lucky outcome (attribution analysis)

→ Content: benchmark return vs. portfolio return chart.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
The Brinson-Hood-Beebower Model
Three effects explain the difference between portfolio and benchmark return:
\[R_p - R_b = \underbrace{AA}_{\text{Allocation}} + \underbrace{SS}_{\text{Selection}} + \underbrace{IA}_{\text{Interaction}}\]
→ Content: attribution formula derivation; intuitive explanation.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Asset Allocation Effect
\[AA_i = (w_{p,i} - w_{b,i}) \times (R_{b,i} - R_b)\]

Did your overweights / underweights add value?
Positive if you overweighted asset classes that outperformed the benchmark
This is the TAA contribution (Lesson 5 decisions)

→ Content: allocation effect table for each IMAG asset class.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Security Selection Effect
\[SS_i = w_{b,i} \times (R_{p,i} - R_{b,i})\]

Did your chosen instruments outperform the asset class benchmark?
Positive if your picked securities beat their benchmark within each class
This is the security selection contribution (Lesson 5 decisions)

→ Content: selection effect table; best and worst contributors.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Reading the Attribution Report
→ Content: annotated screenshot of Cesim attribution output; interpretation guide.






Note


A negative information ratio over multiple periods is a signal to reduce active risk — not increase conviction.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
ESG Performance

How does your portfolio ESG score compare to the benchmark?
Did ESG integration help or hurt financial returns in this period?
Track ESG score evolution over rounds

→ Content: ESG score chart; financial vs. ESG performance scatter plot.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Why a Structured Post-Mortem?

The goal is not to explain away underperformance but to improve the process
Separate: good process / bad outcome from bad process / good outcome
Distinguish luck from skill — especially important in a short-horizon simulation

→ Content: 2×2 process/outcome matrix.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Post-Mortem Framework
For each major decision this round, answer:

What was the decision? (specific weight or security)
What was the rationale? (signal, conviction level)
What was the outcome? (attribution contribution)
Was the process sound? (would you make the same decision again?)
What will you do differently?

→ Content: decision log template; worked example.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Common Behavioural Pitfalls








Bias
Symptom
Antidote




Overconfidence
Excess tracking error
Respect risk limits


Anchoring
Holding losers too long
Pre-commit to stop-loss rules


Herding
Following consensus
Document independent views before group discussion


Recency bias
Over-weighting last quarter
Use long-run CMA as anchor


Confirmation bias
Seeking data that confirms view
Assign devil’s advocate role

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Updating Your IPS & Views

Were any IPS constraints binding? Should they be revised?
Did your CMA forecasts hold? What needs updating?
Are your TAA signals still valid for the next round?
Carry lessons learned into the next round’s decision process

→ Content: IPS review checklist; CMA update worksheet.

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Key Takeaways

Attribution is a diagnostic tool — use it to improve, not to justify
Separate process quality from outcome quality
The feedback loop is what turns a simulation into genuine learning

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Simulation Task — Lesson 6






Final Deliverables


Performance Report:

Calculate TWR, Sharpe, IR for the full simulation period
Complete the attribution table (allocation + selection + interaction)
Compare ESG score to benchmark
Chart portfolio drawdown vs. IPS maximum drawdown limit

Post-Mortem:

Complete the decision log for all rounds
Identify your best and worst decisions (process quality)
List three things you would do differently with hindsight
Prepare a 10-minute team presentation of your investment journey

title: Lesson 6: Performance Measurement & the Feedback Loop
text:
Further Reading

Chapter: Performance Measurement & Attribution
Chapter: Feedback Loop

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

Generate and evaluate short-term macro and factor signals
Translate signals into tactical tilts away from the SAA
Apply fundamental and quantitative approaches to security selection
Execute decisions under liquidity and transaction cost constraints

Teaching chapters: Tactical Research Toolkit · Tactical Asset Allocation & Security Selection · Portfolio Implementation

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
What is Tactical Asset Allocation?

TAA = temporary, deliberate deviations from the SAA
Driven by short-term views on relative asset class attractiveness
Size of tilts is bounded by the risk budget (tracking error limit in IPS)
Horizon: weeks to months — not years (that is the SAA)







Note


A good TAA process starts with the SAA as the neutral position and asks: what do we want to overweight or underweight today?

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Macro Signals

Business cycle indicators: PMI, industrial production, unemployment claims
Monetary policy signals: central bank rate decisions, quantitative tightening
Credit market signals: high-yield spreads, loan officer surveys
Sentiment indicators: consumer confidence, VIX, fund flows

→ Content: IMAG Economic Outlook screen walkthrough; signal extraction exercise.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Factor Signals



Factor
Signal
Typical asset class




Value
Cheap vs. history (CAPE, yield spread)
Equities, credit


Momentum
12-1 month price return
Equities, commodities


Quality
ROE, earnings stability
Equities


Carry
Yield differential
FX, bonds


Low volatility
Realised vol < market
Defensive equities



→ Content: factor return history; current factor signal dashboard.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Signal Aggregation & Scoring

Individual signals are noisy — combine them into a composite score
Simple approach: equal-weight signals after normalisation (z-scores)
Advanced: factor model or machine learning aggregation
Output: a ranked list of asset classes from most to least attractive

→ Content: worked scoring example using IMAG data.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
From Signal to Tilt

Composite score → rank asset classes → derive tilt direction and size
Tilt sizing: proportional to signal strength + inverse of tracking error cost
Constraints: IPS limits on tracking error; no short sales; maximum tilt per asset class

→ Content: tilt derivation table; IPS constraint check.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Risk Budgeting

Total active risk = sum of all tilts’ contribution to tracking error
Each tilt consumes a share of the active risk budget
Prioritise tilts with the highest expected information ratio (IR)

\[IR = \frac{\text{Expected active return}}{\text{Tracking error}}\]
→ Content: risk budget allocation table; marginal contribution calculation.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Overweights & Underweights
→ Content: final TAA tilts table — current round example (to be filled per round).






Team Process



Each analyst presents their top signal
Debate and reach consensus on tilts
Check total tracking error against IPS limit
Document the rationale — you will review it in Lesson 6

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Within-Asset-Class Selection

TAA sets how much to allocate to each asset class
Security selection determines which specific instruments to buy
Adds another layer of active return (and active risk)

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Fundamental Analysis — Equities

Top-down: sector attractiveness → company analysis
Valuation: DCF, price multiples (P/E, EV/EBITDA), dividend yield
Quality screening: return on equity, earnings stability, balance sheet strength
ESG overlay: flag red-rated instruments; apply exclusion list from IPS

→ Content: equity screening template linked to Cesim Analyst screen.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Fundamental Analysis — Fixed Income

Credit analysis: issuer financial health, coverage ratios, debt maturity
Relative value: spread vs. peers and history
Duration positioning: tilt toward short/long depending on rate view
Curve positioning: barbells vs. bullets

→ Content: bond selection scorecard.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Quantitative Screening

Apply factor scores at the individual security level
Rank within each asset class; select top quintile
Combine with ESG filter and liquidity screen
Diversification check: avoid crowding in one sector/issuer

→ Content: quantitative scoring template for IMAG instruments.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
From Decision to Trade

Order types: market, limit, stop
Liquidity: check bid-ask spread and daily volume before sizing orders
Block trades vs. gradual accumulation

→ Content: Cesim order entry walkthrough.

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Transaction Costs

Explicit costs: commissions, stamp duty
Implicit costs: market impact, bid-ask spread
Round-trip cost estimate per asset class
Trade-off: conviction level must exceed transaction cost drag

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Rebalancing vs. Tactical Repositioning

Rebalancing: restore SAA weights after drift (mechanical)
Tactical repositioning: intentional deviation from SAA (active decision)
Netting trades: offset buy and sell orders where possible to reduce costs

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Key Takeaways

TAA adds value only if signals have positive predictive power — be disciplined
Document every tactical decision: signal source, conviction level, expected horizon
Transaction costs are silent return killers — always estimate before trading

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Simulation Task — Lesson 5






Deliverables: TAA + Security Picks


Tactical Asset Allocation:

Score all asset classes using at least two signal types
Derive overweights and underweights (max tilt: per your IPS tracking error limit)
Check total tracking error vs. IPS limit
Submit TAA weights in Cesim → Strategy → Tactical

Security Selection:

Apply fundamental screen to equities: top 3 picks with rationale
Apply credit screen to bonds: preferred maturities and issuers
Confirm all picks meet IPS ESG threshold
Submit selections in Cesim → Strategy → Security Selection

title: Lesson 5: Tactical Allocation & Security Selection
text:
Further Reading

Chapter: Tactical Research Toolkit
Chapter: Tactical Asset Allocation & Security Selection
Chapter: Portfolio Implementation

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

Profile a client and translate qualitative preferences into quantitative objectives
Formulate a complete Investment Policy Statement (IPS)
Define risk tolerance, return requirements, constraints, and ESG policy
Use the IPS as the governing document for all subsequent decisions

Teaching chapters: Client Discovery & Personas · Investment Policy & Risk Governance

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Client Typology

Institutional clients: pension funds, endowments, insurance companies, sovereign wealth funds
Retail clients: high-net-worth individuals, family offices
Key differences: time horizon, liability structure, regulatory constraints, reporting requirements

→ Content: IMAG client persona description (who is the simulated investor?).

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
The Client Discovery Process

Goals: what does the client want to achieve?
Constraints: what limits what they can do?
Preferences: what do they care about beyond returns?
Time horizon: when do they need the money?
Liquidity needs: how much cash must be accessible?

→ Content: worked example with IMAG client profile.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Translating Qualitative to Quantitative



Qualitative statement
Quantitative objective




“Preserve capital”
Max drawdown ≤ 10%; real return ≥ 0%


“Moderate risk taker”
Portfolio volatility 8–12% p.a.


“Long-term growth”
Target return: CPI + 4% over 10 years


“ESG-conscious”
Portfolio ESG score ≥ 7/10



→ Content: IMAG-specific calibration table.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Risk Tolerance Assessment

Risk capacity: ability to absorb losses (financial situation)
Risk willingness: psychological comfort with volatility
Tools: questionnaires, scenario testing, loss aversion probes
Result: a defined risk budget (max volatility, max drawdown, VaR)

→ Content: risk questionnaire template; scoring guide.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
What is an IPS?

The governing document of the portfolio
Agreed upon by client and manager before any investment is made
Serves as the benchmark for every subsequent decision
Must be reviewed at least annually (or after major market events)

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
IPS Structure

Client background & objectives
Return requirement (absolute or relative to benchmark)
Risk tolerance (quantified)
Time horizon
Liquidity requirements
Tax considerations (if applicable)
Legal & regulatory constraints
ESG & exclusion policy
Benchmark definition
Rebalancing policy

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Return Requirement

Required return = spending needs + inflation + fees
Must be realistic given the risk budget
Distinguish: nominal vs. real, absolute vs. relative
Example: “Achieve CPI + 3.5% net of fees over rolling 5-year periods”

→ Content: return decomposition diagram; link to Cesim scoring benchmark.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Risk Constraints

Maximum portfolio volatility (annualised standard deviation)
Maximum drawdown from peak
Tracking error limit vs. benchmark
Value-at-Risk (VaR) at 95% confidence







Note


In IMAG, the risk constraints you set here will govern your SAA (Lesson 4) and all TAA decisions (Lesson 5).

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Constraints: Liquidity, Legal, ESG

Liquidity: minimum cash allocation; redemption notice period
Legal: eligible instruments; prohibited sectors
ESG: minimum portfolio ESG score; explicit exclusions (e.g. coal, weapons)
Concentration: maximum single-issuer or single-sector weight

→ Content: IMAG constraint checklist.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Benchmark Definition

The benchmark is the reference point for relative performance
Choices: market-cap index, custom blended index, CPI-linked target
Must reflect the client’s strategic asset allocation
Defines what is “active risk” vs. “passive market risk”

→ Content: worked example of composite benchmark construction for IMAG.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
The Governance Framework

Who makes decisions? Who approves? Who monitors?
Investment Committee: strategy decisions, IPS approval
Portfolio Manager: day-to-day execution
Risk Officer: limit monitoring, breach escalation
Compliance: regulatory adherence

→ Content: governance org-chart; decision rights matrix.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Monitoring & Reporting

Frequency: daily (risk limits), monthly (performance), quarterly (IPS review)
Key reports: factsheet, attribution report, risk dashboard
Breach protocol: when does a breach trigger a rebalancing?

→ Content: sample reporting template.

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Key Takeaways

The IPS is the most important document you will produce in this course
Every investment decision in Lessons 4–6 must be traceable to the IPS
A well-defined IPS prevents emotion-driven decisions under market stress

title: Lesson 3: Client Discovery & the Investment Policy Statement
text:
Simulation Task — Lesson 3






Deliverable: Your IPS


Draft a complete IPS for the IMAG simulation covering:

Return objective (quantified)
Risk tolerance (max volatility, max drawdown)
Time horizon
Liquidity requirement
ESG policy (minimum score + any exclusions)
Benchmark definition
Rebalancing policy (trigger-based or calendar-based)

Submit before Lesson 4. Your SAA must be consistent with this document.
